content:
# Exodus Chapter 8 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~35r–35v (PDF pp. 68–69); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 48–49 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_008_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_008_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_008_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_008_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

## The Plague of Frogs (vv. 1–15)

**1** And the LORD said to Moses, "Go in to Pharaoh and say to him, 'Thus says the LORD: Send out My people, that they may serve Me.

**2** And if you refuse to send them out, behold, I am striking all your borders with frogs.

**3** And the Nile shall swarm with frogs, and they shall come up and enter into your house, and into your bedchamber, and upon your bed, and into the house of your servants, and upon your people, and into your ovens, and into your kneading troughs.

**4** And upon you and upon your people and upon all your servants the frogs shall come up.'"

**5** And the LORD said to Moses, "Say to Aaron, 'Stretch out your hand with your staff over the rivers, over the canals, and over the pools, and bring up the frogs upon the land of Egypt.'"

**6** And Aaron stretched out his hand over the waters of Egypt, and the frogs came up and covered the land of Egypt.

**7** And the magicians did so with their secret arts, and brought up the frogs upon the land of Egypt.

**8** And Pharaoh called for Moses and for Aaron and said, "Entreat the LORD, that He may remove the frogs from me and from my people, and I will send out the people, that they may sacrifice to the LORD."

**9** And Moses said to Pharaoh, "Glory over me! For when shall I entreat for you, and for your servants, and for your people, to cut off the frogs from you and from your houses — only in the Nile shall they remain?"

**10** And he said, "Tomorrow." And he said, "According to your word, so that you may know that there is none like the LORD our God.

**11** And the frogs shall depart from you, and from your houses, and from your servants, and from your people; only in the Nile shall they remain."

**12** And Moses and Aaron went out from Pharaoh, and Moses cried out to the LORD concerning the matter of the frogs that He had set upon Pharaoh.

**13** And the LORD did according to the word of Moses, and the frogs died — from the houses, from the courtyards, and from the fields.

**14** And they gathered them in heaps upon heaps, and the land stank.

**15** And Pharaoh saw that there was relief, and he made heavy [**kaved**] his heart, and he did not listen to them, as the LORD had spoken.

## The Plague of Gnats (vv. 16–19)

**16** And the LORD said to Moses, "Say to Aaron, 'Stretch out your staff and strike the dust of the earth, and it shall become gnats in all the land of Egypt.'"

**17** And they did so. And Aaron stretched out his hand with his staff and struck the dust of the earth, and the gnats were upon man and upon beast. All the dust of the earth became gnats in all the land of Egypt.

**18** And the magicians did so with their secret arts to bring forth the gnats, but they were not able. And the gnats were upon man and upon beast.

**19** And the magicians said to Pharaoh, "This is the finger of God." But the heart of Pharaoh was strengthened [**chazaq**], and he did not listen to them, as the LORD had spoken.

## The Plague of the Swarm (vv. 20–32)

**20** And the LORD said to Moses, "Rise early in the morning and station yourself before Pharaoh — behold, he goes out to the water — and say to him, 'Thus says the LORD: Send out My people, that they may serve Me.

**21** For if you do not send out My people, behold, I am sending upon you, and upon your servants, and upon your people, and into your houses, the swarm. And the houses of Egypt shall be full of the swarm, and also the ground upon which they stand.

**22** And I will set apart on that day the land of Goshen, upon which My people stand, so that the swarm shall not be there — so that you may know that I am the LORD in the midst of the land.

**23** And I will set a distinction between My people and your people. Tomorrow this sign shall be.'"

**24** And the LORD did so. And a heavy swarm came into the house of Pharaoh, and into the house of his servants, and in all the land of Egypt the land was ruined before the swarm.

**25** And Pharaoh called for Moses and for Aaron and said, "Go, sacrifice to your God in the land."

**26** And Moses said, "It is not right to do so, for the abomination of Egypt we would sacrifice to the LORD our God. Behold, if we sacrifice the abomination of Egypt before their eyes, will they not stone us?

**27** A journey of three days we will go into the wilderness, and we will sacrifice to the LORD our God, as He shall say to us."

**28** And Pharaoh said, "I will send you out, and you shall sacrifice to the LORD your God in the wilderness; only do not go far — going far. Entreat for me."

**29** And Moses said, "Behold, I am going out from you, and I will entreat the LORD, and the swarm shall depart from Pharaoh, from his servants, and from his people tomorrow. Only let not Pharaoh again deal deceitfully by not sending out the people to sacrifice to the LORD."

**30** And Moses went out from Pharaoh and entreated the LORD.

**31** And the LORD did according to the word of Moses, and He removed the swarm from Pharaoh, from his servants, and from his people; not one remained.

**32** And Pharaoh made heavy [**kaved**] his heart also at this time, and he did not send out the people.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations
The Leningrad Codex folio ~35r (PDF p. 68) carries the text forward from Exodus 7, with the opening of what is 8:1 in English versification (7:26 in the Hebrew/MT versification) appearing in the right-hand column. The Hebrew chapter division places 8:1–4 [Eng.] as 7:26–29 [Heb.], with the Hebrew chapter 8 beginning at what is 8:5 in English. This is a well-known versification difference: the MT counts 8:1–28 where the English counts 8:5–32. LOGOS follows English versification throughout for verse numbering. The three-column layout continues with full Tiberian vocalization and accent marks. A setumah (closed paragraph) appears after the frogs section, and a petuchah (open paragraph) before the swarm narrative, reflecting the literary divisions of the plague accounts. Folio ~35v (PDF p. 69) continues through to the end of the chapter and into Exodus 9. Masorah parva notes appear in the margins, including notations on rare forms. The Codex Vaticanus pages 48–49 preserve the LXX text of this chapter. Page 48 carries the latter portion of the frogs narrative, and page 49 (marked with a chapter indicator) continues through the gnats and swarm plagues. The Vaticanus text generally follows the MT's narrative sequence but contains several expansions and minor differences noted below.

### Key Translation Decisions

1. **"I am striking all your borders with frogs" (v. 2):** Hebrew hineh anokhi nogef et-kol-gevulekha batsefarde'im. The participle nogef ("striking") from the root n-g-p conveys imminent, certain action — "I am about to strike." The noun tsefarde'a (plural tsefarde'im) designates the frog. The etymology is debated: some connect it to tsaphar ("to hop") + de'a ("marsh"), yielding "marsh-hopper." The word appears almost exclusively in the Exodus plague narrative and Psalm 78:45/105:30. LOGOS renders "frogs" consistently. The LXX reads ego tuptō panta ta horia sou batrachois ("I strike all your borders with frogs"), using batrachos, the standard Greek word for frog.

2. **"Into your ovens and into your kneading troughs" (v. 3):** Hebrew uvtannurekha uvmish'arotekha. The tannur is the clay oven used for baking bread, and the mish'eret is the kneading trough or bowl where dough is prepared. These domestic details emphasize the totality of the invasion — the frogs penetrate not only living quarters but the very apparatus of food preparation, rendering daily life impossible. The LXX renders eis tous klibанous sou kai eis ta phuramatа sou ("into your ovens and into your kneading troughs"), using precise culinary terms.

3. **"Glory over me!" (v. 9) — hitpa'er alay:** Hebrew hitpa'er alay. This unusual expression has generated extensive discussion. The Hitpael of pa'ar ("to glorify, to beautify oneself") with alay ("over me, upon me") is rendered variously: "Have the honor over me" (i.e., you set the time), "Boast over me" (a courteous concession), or "I defer to you." Moses is granting Pharaoh the privilege of choosing when the frogs will be removed — a bold rhetorical move that demonstrates confidence in divine power. By letting Pharaoh name the time, Moses eliminates any possibility that the removal could be attributed to coincidence. LOGOS renders "Glory over me!" preserving the formal Hebrew construction and its strangeness, with this note to clarify. The LXX renders taxai pros me ("appoint for me [a time]"), interpreting the phrase as an invitation to set a deadline, which captures the pragmatic sense.

4. **"That He had set upon Pharaoh" (v. 12):** Hebrew asher-sam le-Far'oh. The verb sum/sim ("to set, to place") indicates that God deliberately placed the frogs upon Pharaoh as a judicial act. LOGOS preserves the active divine agency. Some translations render "inflicted upon" but "set upon" retains the Hebrew verb's precision.

5. **"From the houses, from the courtyards, and from the fields" (v. 13):** Hebrew min-habattim min-hachatserot umin-hassadot. The threefold min ("from") construction traces the removal from the most intimate (houses) through the intermediate (courtyards) to the most remote (fields) spaces, a literary reversal of the invasion's progress. LOGOS preserves the tripartite structure.

6. **"He made heavy his heart" (v. 15) — kaved, Hiphil:** Hebrew vehakhbed et-libbo. This is the Hiphil (causative) of kaved with Pharaoh as the grammatical subject: Pharaoh himself "made heavy" his own heart. This is the first instance in the plague cycle where Pharaoh is the active agent of his own hardening using kaved. The verb form is causative — he deliberately weighs down his own heart upon seeing the relief. LOGOS renders "made heavy" to reflect the Hiphil agency while maintaining consistency with the kaved root. The LXX renders ebarynеn tēn kardian autou ("he made heavy his heart"), using barunō, the causative form corresponding to the Hiphil.

7. **"The gnats" (vv. 16–18) — kinnim:** Hebrew kinnim (singular kin or kinnah). The identification of this creature is disputed. The MT uses kinnim, which the Talmud (Sanhedrin 67b) identifies as lice, while the LXX renders skniphes/sknipes ("gnats, mosquitoes"). Josephus also supports "lice" (Antiquities 2.14.3). The term may encompass small biting insects generally. LOGOS renders "gnats" following the LXX tradition, as the plague originates from the dust of the earth (v. 16), which better suits gnats/midges that breed in soil than body lice. However, this remains a genuine ambiguity. The Vaticanus text reads skniphes consistently.

8. **"The finger of God" (v. 19):** Hebrew etsba Elohim hi. The magicians' declaration is remarkable — they recognize divine power at work. The "finger" (etsba) denotes minimal divine exertion: what overwhelms Egypt's finest sorcerers is merely God's finger, not His hand or arm. This expression recurs in Exodus 31:18 (the tablets of testimony written by "the finger of God") and is echoed by Jesus in Luke 11:20. Note that the magicians say Elohim ("God"), not YHWH — they acknowledge a divine power but do not name Israel's covenant God. LOGOS preserves "finger of God" without capitalization of "finger," following the Hebrew construct. The LXX renders daktulos theou estin touto ("this is the finger of God").

9. **"The heart of Pharaoh was strengthened" (v. 19) — chazaq:** Hebrew vayechezaq lev Par'oh. Identical to 7:13 and 7:22 — the Qal stative of chazaq with Pharaoh's heart as subject. Despite his magicians' confession, Pharaoh's heart "was strengthened." No divine agent is named. LOGOS renders consistently "was strengthened." The LXX reads kai katischysen hē kardia Pharao ("and the heart of Pharaoh was made strong").

10. **"The swarm" (vv. 20–31) — arov:** Hebrew arov. This term appears only in the plague narratives (Exodus 8 and Psalms 78:45, 105:31). Its meaning is disputed. The root '-r-b means "to mix, to mingle," suggesting a "mixture" or "mixed swarm" of creatures. The Midrash (Exodus Rabbah 11:3) understands it as a mixture of wild beasts. The LXX renders kunomuia ("dog-fly"), a biting fly species — this is the reading preserved clearly in the Vaticanus manuscript on page 49. Josephus also supports flies (Antiquities 2.14.3). Psalm 78:45 says the arov "devoured them" (vayokhlehm), which could support either insects or beasts. LOGOS renders "swarm" without specifying the creature, preserving the Hebrew ambiguity of the term. Where English translations read "flies" (KJV, ESV) or "swarms of flies" (NIV), they are interpreting beyond what the Hebrew text states.

11. **"I will set apart the land of Goshen" (v. 22) — hiphlethi:** Hebrew vehiphleti vayom hahu et-erets Goshen. The Hiphil of palah ("to distinguish, to set apart, to make wonderful") introduces a crucial new element in the plague narrative: geographic discrimination. For the first time, God explicitly distinguishes between the territory of His people and the territory of Egypt. This theological innovation — that plagues can be selective — demonstrates sovereign control over nature. The word peleh/palah is related to "wonder" (pele') — the setting apart is itself a wonder. LOGOS renders "set apart" to capture the Hiphil causative force. The LXX renders paradoxasō ("I will make marvelous/distinguish"), from paradoxazō, preserving the "wonder" dimension of the Hebrew root.

12. **"I will set a distinction" (v. 23) — pedut:** Hebrew vesamti pedut ben ammi uven ammekha. The noun pedut means "ransom, redemption, distinction." Its use here is striking — the "distinction" between Israel and Egypt is described with a word rooted in redemption. Some ancient versions and commentators read peludah ("division, separation"), and the Samaritan Pentateuch reads palut. The MT's pedut, however, carries theological weight: the distinction is itself an act of redemption. LOGOS renders "distinction" but notes the redemptive overtone. The LXX renders diastolēn ("distinction, division"), which captures the separating sense without the redemptive nuance.

13. **"A heavy swarm" (v. 24) — kaved:** Hebrew arov kaved. Here kaved functions as an adjective modifying "swarm" — the swarm is "heavy," that is, massive, severe, weighty. This is the same root used for heart-hardening (vv. 15, 32), creating a literary resonance: Pharaoh makes his heart "heavy" while a "heavy" swarm descends. LOGOS renders "heavy swarm" to preserve this wordplay.

14. **"The abomination of Egypt" (v. 26) — to'avat Mitsrayim:** Hebrew to'avat Mitsrayim. Moses' argument is that Israel's sacrificial animals (particularly cattle, sheep, and goats) are sacred or abominable to the Egyptians. The noun to'evah ("abomination, detestable thing") here refers to what Egyptians find sacrilegious. The genitive can be read as objective ("what Egypt finds abominable" — i.e., the Egyptians would be horrified to see their sacred animals slaughtered) or subjective ("the abominable practice of Egypt" — i.e., Egyptian religious customs). The context favors the objective reading: sacrificing cattle before the Egyptians would provoke violence. This explains why Israel must go three days into the wilderness. LOGOS renders "the abomination of Egypt" preserving the ambiguity. The LXX renders ta bdelugmata tōn Aiguptiōn ("the abominations of the Egyptians").

15. **"Only do not go far — going far" (v. 28):** Hebrew raq harchek lo-tarchiku lalechet. The infinitive absolute harchek with the finite verb tarchiku creates emphasis: "only do not go far, going far" — that is, "only you must absolutely not go far." The infinitive absolute construction intensifies the prohibition. Pharaoh grants permission to sacrifice but with a geographical leash. LOGOS preserves the awkward doubling of the infinitive absolute construction to signal the Hebrew emphasis. The LXX renders makran mē apoteinēte poreuthēnai ("do not stretch out far to go").

16. **"Pharaoh made heavy his heart" (v. 32) — kaved, Hiphil:** Hebrew vayakhbed Par'oh et-libbo gam bapa'am hazot. Again the Hiphil of kaved with Pharaoh as active agent: "Pharaoh made heavy his heart." The addition of gam bapa'am hazot ("also at this time") marks this as a repeated, deliberate act. LOGOS renders consistently with v. 15. The LXX renders ebarunen Pharao tēn kardian autou kai epi tou kairou toutou ("Pharaoh made heavy his heart also at this time").

### Hardening Verb Tracking — Exodus 8

| Verse | Hebrew Root | Form | Subject/Agent | LOGOS Rendering |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 8:15 | **kaved** | Hiphil (causative) | Pharaoh makes his own heart heavy | "he made heavy his heart" |
| 8:19 | **chazaq** | Qal (stative) | Pharaoh's heart was strengthened | "the heart of Pharaoh was strengthened" |
| 8:32 | **kaved** | Hiphil (causative) | Pharaoh makes his own heart heavy | "Pharaoh made heavy his heart" |

*Note: In chapter 8, the hardening pattern shifts significantly. The root kaved dominates, appearing twice (vv. 15, 32), both times in the Hiphil with Pharaoh as the active agent — he deliberately makes his own heart heavy. Chazaq appears once (v. 19) in the familiar Qal stative (heart as subject, no divine agent named). Qashah does not appear. The chapter thus presents hardening as primarily Pharaoh's own act: upon seeing relief from the frogs (v. 15), he actively weighs down his heart; after the swarm is removed (v. 32), he does it again. The chazaq instance in v. 19 is particularly striking because it follows the magicians' own confession that "this is the finger of God" — even his advisors recognize divine power, yet Pharaoh's heart remains firm. The cumulative pattern across chapters 7–8 shows a progression: divine announcement of hardening (7:3, qashah), then the heart strengthening itself without named agent (7:13, 22; 8:19, chazaq), then Pharaoh actively hardening his own heart (8:15, 32, kaved Hiphil). God's active hardening of Pharaoh's heart (chazaq Piel with God as subject) has still not occurred — that begins in 9:12.*

### Cumulative Hardening Verb Table — Exodus 7–8

| Verse | Root | Form | Agent | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 7:3 | qashah | Hiphil | God (prospective) | "I will make hard" — announced, not yet enacted |
| 7:13 | chazaq | Qal | Heart (stative) | "was strengthened" — after staff sign |
| 7:14 | kaved | Adjective | — (descriptive) | "is heavy" — God's diagnosis |
| 7:22 | chazaq | Qal | Heart (stative) | "was strengthened" — after blood plague |
| 8:15 | kaved | Hiphil | Pharaoh (active) | "made heavy" — after frogs removed |
| 8:19 | chazaq | Qal | Heart (stative) | "was strengthened" — after gnats; magicians confess |
| 8:32 | kaved | Hiphil | Pharaoh (active) | "made heavy" — after swarm removed |

### Magicians (Chartummim) Tracking — Exodus 8

| Verse | Action | Result |
|---|---|---|
| 8:7 | Replicate frogs with secret arts (lahateyhem) | Successful — but only adds to the problem |
| 8:18 | Attempt to produce gnats with secret arts | **Failure** — "they were not able" |
| 8:19 | Confess to Pharaoh | "This is the finger of God" (etsba Elohim) |

*Note: Chapter 8 marks the turning point for Egypt's magicians. In 7:11–12, they replicated the staff-to-serpent sign; in 7:22, they replicated the blood. In 8:7, they replicate the frogs — but this is absurd, as they merely add to Egypt's misery rather than alleviating it. Then in 8:18, they fail entirely: they cannot produce gnats. Their confession in 8:19 — "the finger of God" — acknowledges a power beyond their art. After this point, the magicians play no further role in the plague narrative until 9:11, where they cannot even stand before Moses because of the boils. Their trajectory is one of progressive humiliation: imitation, futile imitation, failure, confession, and finally personal affliction.*

### Key Term Renderings — Exodus 8

| Hebrew | LOGOS Rendering | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| tsefarde'a / tsefarde'im | frogs | vv. 1–14; possibly "marsh-hoppers" etymologically |
| kinnim | gnats | vv. 16–18; LXX skniphes; Talmud: lice; debated |
| arov | swarm | vv. 20–31; root '-r-b "to mix"; LXX kunomuia "dog-fly" |
| etsba Elohim | finger of God | v. 19; minimal divine exertion |
| to'evah | abomination | v. 26; what Egypt finds sacrilegious |
| pedut | distinction | v. 23; root p-d-h "to redeem"; redemptive overtone |
| chartummim | magicians | vv. 7, 18–19; Egyptian lector priests |
| lahatim / belahateyhem | secret arts | vv. 7, 18; enchantments, magical concealment |
| tannur | oven | v. 3; clay baking oven |
| mish'eret | kneading trough | v. 3; dough bowl |
| hitpa'er | glory / boast | v. 9; "Glory over me!" — concession of honor |
| palah (Hiphil) | set apart | v. 22; to distinguish, make wonderful |

---

### Versification Note
The Hebrew (MT) and English versification differ in Exodus 8. The MT counts 7:26–29 where English counts 8:1–4, and the MT's chapter 8 begins at what is 8:5 in English, running through 8:28 [Heb.] = 8:32 [Eng.]. LOGOS follows English versification (32 verses) throughout this translation, consistent with standard English Bible conventions. The verse content is identical; only the numbering differs.

---

*Note on the LXX (Vaticanus):* The Vaticanus text of Exodus 8 (pages 48–49) follows the MT's narrative closely in overall structure. Several distinctive readings are noted above. The LXX's rendering of arov as kunomuia ("dog-fly") is an interpretive translation that specifies what the Hebrew leaves ambiguous. The LXX version of Moses' "Glory over me" (v. 9) as taxai pros me ("appoint for me") similarly interprets an obscure Hebrew expression. In v. 22, the LXX's paradoxasō ("I will make marvelous") for the Hiphil of palah captures the "wonder" dimension of the Hebrew root that English "set apart" does not fully convey. The Vaticanus manuscript on page 49 shows a clear chapter/section marker (indicated by an ekthesis or marginal notation) at the transition to the swarm plague, corresponding to the petuchah in the MT. The magicians' confession in v. 19 reads daktulos theou estin touto in Vaticanus, matching the MT precisely. Overall, the LXX of chapter 8 is a faithful rendering that diverges mainly in lexical choices (specifying ambiguous terms) rather than in narrative content or theological emphasis.

--- LOGOS
